subject: ap-government
title: Concept Application — Kennedy Space Speech and NASA
stimulus:
In 1958, President Dwight D. Eisenhower signed the National Aeronautics and Space Act, establishing the National Aeronautics and Space Administration (NASA) as an independent agency within the federal bureaucracy responsible for aeronautics, space research, and exploration.

On September 12, 1962, President John F. Kennedy addressed a stadium crowd of 40,000 people at Rice University. The speech, officially titled "We Choose to Go to the Moon," came just one year after the Soviet Union launched the first person into space and five years after the Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the first satellite into space. The purpose of the speech was to increase support for Kennedy's proposal to launch a mission to the Moon and explain why this program needed to be a high budget priority. The event was the lead story in The New York Times and was covered in newspapers around the country.

President Kennedy talked about scientific progress and how the space mission represented a major step forward for humankind: "We choose to go to the Moon in this decade . . . not because [it is] easy, but because [it is] hard. . . ."

Landing a person on the Moon represented an enormous challenge, and many doubted it could be done within a decade. But on July 20, 1969, NASA astronaut Neil Armstrong became the first person to step foot on the Moon's surface.

parts.prompt: Describe the informal power used by the president in the scenario.
parts.scoring_notes: Accept 'bully pulpit,' 'power to persuade,' or 'going public' as the informal power. The response must describe what the power involves, not just name it.

scoring_points.description: Describe the informal power used by the president in the scenario.

required_elements: By giving this speech, President Kennedy used the power to persuade by directly communicating with the public and gain support for his agenda.
alternatives.correct_example: By giving this speech, President Kennedy used the power to persuade by directly communicating with the public and gain support for his agenda.

required_elements: The president used the bully pulpit when he gave his speech.
alternatives.correct_example: The president used the bully pulpit when he gave his speech.

wrong_examples: The president signed a bill into law. (Describes a formal/constitutional power — the scenario is about a public speech, which is an informal power)
Kennedy used executive orders to start the space program. (Not supported by the scenario — describes a formal power not referenced in the stimulus)
common_traps: The bully pulpit and power to persuade are informal powers — they are not listed in Article II of the Constitution; if you cite a formal power like the veto or executive orders you will not earn this point
The response should DESCRIBE the informal power (what it involves), not merely name it — explain that it involves using the presidency's visibility and platform to shape public opinion
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for describing the informal power used by the president in the scenario. Acceptable descriptions include: By giving this speech, President Kennedy used the power to persuade by directly communicating with the public and gain support for his agenda. The president used the bully pulpit when he gave his speech.

sample_responses.response: By giving this speech, President Kennedy used the power to persuade by directly communicating with the public to gain support for his agenda.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies and describes the bully pulpit / power to persuade as an informal presidential power used in the scenario. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: President Kennedy used his executive power to direct NASA to pursue the moon mission.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a formal/constitutional power rather than the informal power (bully pulpit) shown in the scenario — the scenario focuses on a public speech to build popular support, not a directive. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: In the context of the scenario, explain how the power described in part A could have impacted Congressional oversight.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must explain a specific effect on congressional oversight — either increasing or explaining the dynamic. Accept either direction (encouraging oversight or creating pressure that shapes oversight).

scoring_points.description: In the context of the scenario, explain how the power described in part A could have impacted Congressional oversight.

required_elements: The president's speech could have encouraged voters to pressure Congress to oversee implementation of the president's agenda, like holding a hearing on space exploration.
alternatives.correct_example: The president's speech could have encouraged voters to pressure Congress to oversee implementation of the president's agenda, like holding a hearing on space exploration.

required_elements: By bringing more attention to his agenda, the president's speech could have encouraged opponents in Congress to call for greater oversight of NASA's activities.
alternatives.correct_example: By bringing more attention to his agenda, the president's speech could have encouraged opponents in Congress to call for greater oversight of NASA's activities.

wrong_examples: Congress would have to follow Kennedy's agenda. (Incorrect — Congress is a co-equal branch and is not required to follow the president's agenda simply because of a public speech)
The speech would convince Congress to pass a space budget. (Describes legislative action, not congressional oversight — oversight involves monitoring and investigating executive actions)
common_traps: Congressional oversight is distinct from lawmaking — oversight involves hearings, investigations, and monitoring of the executive branch; do not confuse oversight with passing legislation

The connection must run through the scenario: Kennedy used the bully pulpit → public attention increased → Congress was pressured to (a) support/fund the mission or (b) scrutinize NASA's spending and implementation
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the power described in part A could have impacted Congressional oversight. Acceptable explanations include: The president's speech could have encouraged voters to pressure Congress to oversee implementation of the president's agenda, like holding a hearing on space exploration. By bringing more attention to his agenda, the president's speech could have encouraged opponents in Congress to call for greater oversight of NASA's activities.

sample_responses.response: The president's speech could have encouraged voters to pressure Congress to oversee implementation of the president's agenda, such as holding hearings to ensure NASA was spending its budget efficiently on the moon mission.
sample_responses.commentary: Connects the bully pulpit to congressional oversight by showing the chain: public attention increased → voters pressured Congress → Congress held hearings. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Congress would have been persuaded by the speech to increase NASA's budget.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes a legislative action (budget appropriation) rather than congressional oversight (monitoring and investigating executive actions). Does not earn the point. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain how NASA could have impacted the implementation of the president's agenda described in the scenario.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must focus on NASA's bureaucratic powers (discretion, rulemaking) and how they affect implementation — not just that NASA 'would carry out' the mission.

scoring_points.description: Explain how NASA could have impacted the implementation of the president's agenda described in the scenario.

required_elements: By using its discretionary authority, NASA could have chosen how to best carry out the mission to the moon.
alternatives.correct_example: By using its discretionary authority, NASA could have chosen how to best carry out the mission to the moon.

required_elements: NASA could have used its rulemaking authority to create the regulations that the mission to the moon will follow.
alternatives.correct_example: NASA could have used its rulemaking authority to create the regulations that the mission to the moon will follow.

required_elements: The bureaucracy could have used its discretionary and rulemaking authority to address President Kennedy's policy agenda.
alternatives.correct_example: The bureaucracy could have used its discretionary and rulemaking authority to address President Kennedy's policy agenda.

wrong_examples: NASA would implement the president's wishes. (Too vague — does not explain the specific bureaucratic powers or mechanisms involved)
NASA could refuse to carry out the mission. (While bureaucratic resistance is possible, the response must explain bureaucratic power — discretion or rulemaking — not just whether NASA would comply)
common_traps: The key AP Gov concept here is bureaucratic discretion and rulemaking — NASA as an independent agency fills in the details of legislation and presidential directives through rule-making and expert judgment; just saying NASA 'would do the work' does not explain how bureaucratic power shapes implementation
Bureaucratic delay or lack of expertise can HINDER implementation — a response noting that NASA's technical limitations could slow progress is also acceptable
scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how NASA could have impacted the implementation of the president's agenda. Acceptable explanations include: By using its discretionary authority, NASA could have chosen how to best carry out the mission to the moon. NASA could have used its rulemaking authority to create the regulations that the mission to the moon will follow. The bureaucracy could have used its discretionary and rulemaking authority to address President Kennedy's policy agenda.

sample_responses.response: By using its discretionary authority, NASA could have chosen how to best carry out the mission to the moon — determining which technologies to develop, which contractors to hire, and which mission profiles to pursue, giving NASA significant control over the actual implementation of Kennedy's agenda.
sample_responses.commentary: Explains bureaucratic discretion as the mechanism by which NASA shapes policy implementation. Goes beyond just saying NASA 'would do the work.' Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: NASA would implement Kennedy's wishes and launch the mission.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — does not explain the specific bureaucratic powers (discretion, rulemaking) that give NASA influence over implementation. Just saying NASA 'would do the work' does not earn the point. 0 pts.
